title: Influence of critical parameters of nanosuspension formulation on the permeability of a poorly soluble drug through the skin--a case study.

anchor_evidence.locator: Block 64: "the skin was mounted on static vertical Franz Diffusion cells—Permegear Inc., Bethlehem, PA (receptor volume 5.1 ml, donor area 0.64 cm²)"
anchor_evidence.snippet: Block 64: "the skin was mounted on static vertical Franz Diffusion cells—Permegear Inc., Bethlehem, PA (receptor volume 5.1 ml, donor area 0.64 cm²)"

anchor_evidence.locator: Block 104 (Table II) and Block 102 (narrative); cumulative permeation at 24, 48, 72 h and flux values
anchor_evidence.snippet: The cumulative amount of drug permeated through the skin after 24, 48, and 72 h is shown in Table II. Flux values were determined for each permeation study (Table II)... receptor samples were collected at predetermined time points (4, 8, 12, 24, 36, 48, 72 h)

As shown in Table I, several formulations were evaluated using nanosuspensions... We used different types of gel forming polymers at three different concentrations (Table I). The overall study design is shown in Table II.

notes: Primary IVPT study using ibuprofen as model drug in nanosuspension gel formulations. Barriers include both dermatomed porcine skin (DOE study, Blocks 82/102) and human skin (optimized formulation, Block 117). Static vertical Franz diffusion cells (Permegear, 5.1 mL receptor, 0.64 cm² donor area), PBS pH 7.4 receptor at 37°C, 600 RPM. Time points: 4, 8, 12, 24, 36, 48, 72 h. Endpoints (cumulative permeation and flux) reported in Table II (main text). Formulation compositions reported in Table I. 2³ factorial design used to assess effects of particle size, vitamin E TPGS concentration, and gel polymer concentration on flux.
